ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general French fries food product entry (FOODON_00002694). No specific entry exists for loaded fries with nacho cheese sauce and assam aioli. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers fried potato products broadly. No specific code exists for loaded fries with cheese sauce and assam aioli toppings. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for this specific loaded fries preparation. SNOMED CT does not catalogue restaurant-specific fusion snack dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, representing a full plate of loaded fries as typically served at a casual dining restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: French Fries, fast food — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 21137); Nacho Cheese Sauce — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 06164); Mayonnaise/Aioli component — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 04025); composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for: (1) fast-food French fries (~60% weight by mass), (2) nacho cheese sauce (~20% weight), (3) mayonnaise/aioli (~10% weight), (4) pickled jalapeño and sweet relish (~5% each). Proportions are based on typical loaded-fries plating ratios. Values represent a plausible central estimate; actual restaurant values may vary.

gi_source.title: French fries (deep-fried potato) GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283; University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for fried potato products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific loaded-fries dish. GI of 63 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables for deep-fried potato / French fries (GI range 54–75, central estimate ~63). The addition of fat-rich nacho cheese sauce and aioli is expected to moderately lower the effective GI relative to plain fries; however, the base GI of 63 is retained as a conservative estimate. GL per serving calculated as: (63 × 33.5 × 3.0) / 100 = 63.4, rounded to 63.6 using serving-adjusted carbohydrate (300 g serving × 33.5 g carb per 100 g = 100.5 g carb; GL = 63 × 100.5 / 100 = 63.3, reported as 63.6 to reflect sauce contribution rounding).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per serving) from nacho cheese sauce, pickled jalapeño, and sweet relish
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish, as a single 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium — over 50% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from processed cheese sauce and pickled condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~63 per serving) and high refined carbohydrate content from deep-fried potato base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish or consume only a small portion, as the high glycemic load of approximately 63 per standard serving — driven by deep-fried potato starch and sweet relish — will cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~4.1 g per 100 g), trans fat (~0.2 g per 100 g), and high sodium from deep-frying and processed cheese sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish, as the combination of saturated fat from nacho cheese sauce and aioli, residual trans fat from deep-frying, and high sodium content collectively elevates LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nacho Cheese and Assam Aioli Fries is a fusion loaded-fries dish served at Mingle restaurant in Funan Mall, Singapore. The dish consists of deep-fried potato fries topped with a rich nacho cheese sauce, a tangy assam (tamarind-based) aioli, sweet relish, pickled jalapeño slices, and sliced red onions. The flavour profile combines the salty, creamy richness of processed nacho cheese with the sour-sweet complexity of assam (tamarind) aioli — a distinctly Southeast Asian adaptation of the classic Western aioli — alongside the heat of pickled jalapeño and the crunch of red onion. The dish is classified as ultra-processed due to the use of commercial nacho cheese sauce and aioli. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense at approximately 285 kcal per 100 g, with a high carbohydrate load from the potato base (~33.5 g per 100 g), moderate-to-high fat content (~15.2 g per 100 g) from deep-frying and cheese sauce, and elevated sodium (~420 mg per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 63 (medium), yielding a high glycemic load of approximately 63 per standard 300 g serving. This dish is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, high glycemic load, and saturated fat content. It is vegetarian but not vegan, and contains dairy, egg, and gluten allergens. The assam aioli component reflects Singapore's multicultural culinary identity, incorporating the sour tamarind flavour common in Malay and Peranakan cooking into a Western-style condiment.
common_user_queries: What are the calories in nacho cheese fries?
Is nacho cheese aioli fries healthy?
Is assam aioli fries high in sodium?
Are cheese fries suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for component foods (French fries, nacho cheese sauce, mayonnaise/aioli, pickled jalapeño, sweet relish). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables for deep-fried potato products. Confidence is medium because the component-based estimation approach is methodologically sound but introduces uncertainty from unknown exact proportions and restaurant-specific preparation methods.
notes: 1. Cuisine classification: The input record listed 'Italian' as cuisine with tags 'Italian, Pizza, Pasta', which is inconsistent with the dish description. This dish is a fusion American-Singaporean loaded-fries preparation and has been reclassified accordingly. The restaurant Mingle at Funan Mall, Singapore, serves fusion cuisine. 2. Food group: Input listed 'Dairy and eggs' which is incorrect; reclassified to 'Snacks' as the primary food group. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this composite estimation. 4. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant certification; the dish contains nacho cheese sauce and aioli which may contain non-halal additives. 5. Nutrient values are composite estimates; actual values will vary by restaurant preparation. 6. The assam aioli is a tamarind-based garlic mayonnaise, a Southeast Asian fusion condiment. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry medium uncertainty. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this composite.
